subtitle: Two major NEJM trials challenge long-standing ICU habits: liberal fluid resuscitation in septic shock, and routine bicarbonate correction for metabolic acidosis.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Hello, and welcome to your Critical Care Daily briefing for July second, twenty twenty-six.
Today we're looking at two major randomized trials, both published in the New England Journal of Medicine and both focused on resuscitation strategies in critically ill patients.
First, a large Australian and New Zealand trial asks whether liberal fluids or early vasopressors work better in septic shock.
Second, we cover a multinational trial testing whether sodium bicarbonate protects the kidneys in patients with metabolic acidosis who are on vasopressors.
Let's get into it.

script.heading: Story 1 — Vasopressors or Fluids in Septic Shock
sentences: Our first story is the ARISE FLUIDS trial, published online on June eleventh in the New England Journal of Medicine.
The clinical question is one every emergency and ICU clinician faces: in early septic shock, should you give more intravenous fluid, or start vasopressors sooner while holding back on fluid?
For years, guidelines have recommended around thirty milliliters per kilogram of fluid up front, but there's growing concern that too much fluid can cause harm, including pulmonary edema.
This trial, run by the ARISE FLUIDS Investigators together with the ANZICS and ACEM clinical trial networks, randomized adult patients presenting to the emergency department with septic shock.

One thousand patients underwent randomization: four hundred ninety-nine to a vasopressor-first strategy with restricted fluid volumes, and five hundred one to a strategy of higher fluid volumes with later vasopressors.
After excluding patients for whom consent could not be obtained, nine hundred sixty-three patients made up the intention-to-treat population.
In the first twenty-four hours, the vasopressor group received about eleven hundred milliliters less intravenous fluid than the fluids group.
The vasopressor group also had eighteen-point-nine percentage points more patients actually receiving vasopressors.
The primary outcome was days alive and out of hospital through day ninety.
The result: a median of seventy-six days in both groups, with a difference of exactly zero days and a confidence interval crossing zero, so there was no significant difference between strategies.

However, there was one striking safety signal: pulmonary edema occurred in only zero-point-six percent of the vasopressor group, compared with five percent of the fluids group, a highly significant difference.
So while neither strategy beat the other on the main survival-and-discharge outcome, the vasopressor-first approach caused meaningfully less fluid overload.

A limitation is that the trial excluded patients with contraindications to early vasopressors and was conducted mainly in well-resourced Australian and New Zealand emergency departments, which may limit how well it generalizes elsewhere.
It was also an open-label trial, so clinician behavior around fluid titration could have influenced some of the secondary findings.

The take-home is that early vasopressors with restricted fluids appear at least as good as a liberal fluid strategy for survival and hospital-free days, and they meaningfully reduce the risk of pulmonary edema, supporting a shift away from routine large-volume fluid boluses in septic shock.

script.heading: Story 2 — Sodium Bicarbonate for Metabolic Acidosis and Shock
sentences: Our second story is the SODa-BIC trial, published in the New England Journal of Medicine on June twelfth, just one day after the ARISE FLUIDS results.
This trial tackles a decades-old ICU debate: should we give sodium bicarbonate to critically ill patients with severe metabolic acidosis who are already on vasopressors?
Acidemia is common in shock and is linked with worse organ function and higher mortality, but whether correcting the pH with bicarbonate actually helps patients has never been clearly proven.
This was a pragmatic, adaptive, double-blind, randomized trial run across fifty-five intensive care units in seven countries.
Investigators enrolled adults with a blood pH below seven-point-three-zero and a base excess of minus four millimoles per liter or lower, who were already receiving vasopressors in the ICU.

Five hundred patients were enrolled: two hundred forty-five received sodium bicarbonate, and two hundred fifty-five received a placebo of five percent dextrose, infused for up to five hours and titrated toward a target pH.
The primary outcome was a major adverse kidney event within thirty days, a composite of death, need for renal-replacement therapy, or persistent kidney dysfunction.

The result: a major adverse kidney event occurred in forty-point-two percent of the bicarbonate group, versus thirty-nine-point-four percent of the placebo group, an adjusted difference of just one-point-two percentage points, which was not statistically significant.

Renal-replacement therapy was actually used somewhat less often in the bicarbonate group, sixteen-point-eight percent versus twenty-point-nine percent, though this difference also did not reach statistical significance.
In-hospital mortality by day thirty was twenty-five-point-four percent with bicarbonate and twenty-four percent with placebo, essentially the same.

A small safety signal appeared: four patients, or one-point-six percent, in the bicarbonate group had a reported adverse effect, compared with none in the placebo group, though this too fell short of statistical significance.
So overall, correcting the pH with bicarbonate did not translate into better kidney outcomes or better survival.
One limitation is that bicarbonate was infused for only up to five hours, so the trial doesn't tell us whether more prolonged or more aggressive correction would behave differently.
It's also possible that a subgroup, such as patients with more severe acute kidney injury who already need renal support, could still benefit, a question the investigators say deserves further study.

The take-home is that routine sodium bicarbonate for vasopressor-dependent patients with metabolic acidosis does not appear to reduce kidney injury or death, so clinicians should not expect a survival benefit from correcting pH alone in this population.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's briefing.

Both trials share a common thread: two long-standing ICU habits, liberal fluid resuscitation and routine bicarbonate correction, are being tested against high-quality evidence and coming up short as universal strategies.
As always, these results should be weighed alongside your local protocols and each patient's individual clinical picture.
Thanks for listening, and we'll be back tomorrow with more critical care news.

articles.title: Vasopressors or Fluids in Early Septic Shock (ARISE FLUIDS)
articles.type: Multicenter randomized controlled trial (n = 1000 randomized, 963 intention-to-treat)
articles.summary: The ARISE FLUIDS Investigators, with the ANZICS and ACEM trial networks, randomized adult emergency department patients with septic shock to either restricted-fluid-volume-plus-early-vasopressors (n=499) or higher-fluid-volume-plus-later-vasopressors (n=501) for 6 to 24 hours. In the intention-to-treat population (n=963), the vasopressor group received about 1108 mL less IV fluid in the first 24 hours (95% CI -1395 to -850) and had 18.9 percentage points higher vasopressor use (95% CI 13.3-24.5). The primary outcome, median days alive and out of hospital to day 90, was identical at 76 days in both groups (difference 0.0 days; 95% CI -2.7 to 2.7; P=1.00). Pulmonary edema occurred in 0.6% of the vasopressor group versus 5.0% of the fluids group (P<0.001).
articles.analysis: This is one of the largest head-to-head tests of early vasopressors versus liberal fluids in septic shock, and it found no difference in the primary outcome, days alive and out of the hospital at 90 days. That neutral result, paired with an eightfold reduction in pulmonary edema in the vasopressor group, is clinically meaningful: it suggests a restrictive-fluid, early-vasopressor approach is at least as safe and effective as traditional large-volume resuscitation, while avoiding a measurable harm. Limitations include its open-label design (risk of care pattern bias), enrollment limited to well-resourced Australian and New Zealand EDs (generalizability to lower-resource settings is unclear), and exclusion of patients with contraindications to early vasopressors (e.g., limited IV access for peripheral vasopressor administration). For practice, this adds to a growing body of evidence (alongside trials like CLOVERS) supporting more conservative fluid strategies with earlier vasopressor initiation in septic shock, particularly to reduce fluid-overload complications, without a signal of harm to mortality or hospital-free days.

articles.title: Sodium Bicarbonate for Critically Ill Adults with Metabolic Acidosis and Shock (SODa-BIC)
articles.type: Pragmatic, adaptive, double-blind, multinational randomized controlled trial (n = 500)
articles.summary: The SODa-BIC Investigators (Australian and New Zealand Intensive Care Society Clinical Trials Group) randomized 500 adults across 55 ICUs in 7 countries with metabolic acidosis (pH <7.30, base excess ≤ -4 mmol/L) receiving vasopressors to sodium bicarbonate (n=245) or placebo/5% dextrose (n=255), infused up to 5 hours and titrated to pH ≥7.30. The primary outcome, a major adverse kidney event (death, renal-replacement therapy, or persistent renal dysfunction) within 30 days, occurred in 40.2% of the bicarbonate group versus 39.4% of placebo (adjusted difference 1.2 percentage points; 95% CI -7.1 to 9.4; P=0.78). Renal-replacement therapy use was 16.8% vs 20.9% (95% CI -10.6 to 2.7), and 30-day in-hospital mortality was 25.4% vs 24.0% (95% CI -5.6 to 9.2). Four bicarbonate patients (1.6%) had an adverse effect versus none on placebo (P=0.06).
articles.analysis: SODa-BIC directly tests a common ICU reflex: correcting acidemia with bicarbonate in vasopressor-dependent patients. The trial found no benefit on the composite kidney outcome, on renal-replacement therapy use, or on mortality, despite adequate power (500 patients across a diverse international cohort) and a pragmatic, clinically representative protocol. The confidence intervals are reasonably tight around a null effect, making a clinically important benefit unlikely, though they don't fully exclude a small effect in either direction. Limitations include the short 5-hour infusion window, meaning the trial cannot address more prolonged bicarbonate strategies, and the composite primary outcome, which blends mortality with softer renal endpoints. For practice, this reinforces recent evidence (including the earlier BICAR-ICU trial) that routine bicarbonate correction should not be viewed as a treatment that improves survival or kidney outcomes in shock; it may still have a role in specific severe acidemia scenarios (e.g., life-threatening hyperkalemia or extreme acidemia), but clinicians should not expect it to change the trajectory of organ failure or death in general vasopressor-dependent, acidotic ICU patients.
